name: chembl-database
description: Query ChEMBL bioactive molecules and drug discovery data. Search compounds by structure/properties, retrieve bioactivity data (IC50, Ki), find inhibitors, perform SAR studies, for medicinal chemistry.
qualityReason: Highly specific with clear use cases
capabilities: Search molecules by name, SMILES structure, or physicochemical properties
Retrieve bioactivity data including IC50, Ki, EC50, and Kd values
Find similar compounds and perform substructure searches
Query drug information, mechanisms of action, and clinical indications
Export query results to pandas DataFrames for analysis
author: K-Dense-AI
securitySummary: All 198 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The analyzer misidentified markdown code formatting (backticks) as shell command execution, SMILES chemical notation as shell operators, and documentation URLs as network endpoints. This is a legitimate scientific database integration skill containing only documentation for the ChEMBL Web Resource Client library. The Python example file contains only wrapper functions that call the official chembl_webresource_client library. No executable malicious code or dangerous functionality exists.
valueStatement: Access the ChEMBL database to search compounds by structure and properties. Retrieve bioactivity measurements, find inhibitors, and perform structure-activity relationship studies for drug discovery research.
userTitle: Query ChEMBL bioactive molecules and drug discovery data
limitations: Rate limited by ChEMBL API fair usage policies
Cannot access local databases - only queries the ChEMBL web API
Data completeness depends on ChEMBL curation status

useCases.title: Find Inhibitors for Drug Targets
useCases.description: Search for potent inhibitors of specific targets like EGFR or kinases by querying bioactivity measurements.

useCases.title: Analyze Drug-Target Interactions
useCases.description: Retrieve drug mechanisms, indications, and activity profiles to understand compound-target relationships.

useCases.title: Structure-Activity Relationship Studies
useCases.description: Find similar compounds and analyze molecular properties to identify structure-activity patterns.

promptTemplates.title: Find Molecule by ID
promptTemplates.prompt: Get information about molecule CHEMBL25 (aspirin) from ChEMBL

promptTemplates.title: Search by Properties
promptTemplates.prompt: Find all molecules with molecular weight under 500, LogP under 5, and at most 10 hydrogen bond acceptors

promptTemplates.title: Query Bioactivity
promptTemplates.scenario: Find potent compounds for a target
promptTemplates.prompt: Find all EGFR inhibitors with IC50 values below 100 nM

promptTemplates.title: Structure Search
promptTemplates.prompt: Find compounds similar to aspirin (CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O) with 85 percent similarity

bestPractices: Use caching to respect rate limits when making repeated queries
Apply molecular weight and LogP filters early to reduce result sets
Always check the data_validity_comment field for potential issues
antiPatterns: Making rapid consecutive requests without respecting rate limits
Ignoring the pchembl_value normalization when comparing activities
Assuming all compounds in ChEMBL are experimentally verified

faq.question: What is ChEMBL?
faq.answer: ChEMBL is a manually curated database of bioactive molecules maintained by EBI, containing over 2 million compounds.

faq.question: Does this skill require API keys?
faq.answer: No, ChEMBL is a free public database. No authentication is required for basic queries.

faq.question: What activity values are available?
faq.answer: ChEMBL provides IC50, Ki, Kd, EC50, and other bioactivity measurements with standardized units.

faq.question: Can I search by chemical structure?
faq.answer: Yes, use SMILES strings for similarity searches and substructure matching.

faq.question: How are results returned?
faq.answer: Results are lazy-evaluated iterators. Convert to list() to force execution.

faq.question: What are good property filters for drug-likeness?
faq.answer: MW under 500, LogP under 5, HBA under 10, HBD under 5 follow Lipinski's rule of 5.
